summary: Gruber analyzes China's ban on iPhones for government employees, exploring the geopolitical tensions between Apple and the Chinese government. He argues the ban likely stems from both nationalism and the CCP's frustration that iPhone security is beyond their surveillance reach. He frames the Apple-China relationship as mutually dependent: Apple needs Chinese manufacturing, but China gains enormous prestige from producing the world's most premium consumer products. Gruber contends that China risks undermining its own reputation as a trustworthy manufacturing partner by antagonizing Apple. He dismisses Huawei as a viable competitor and argues Apple holds more leverage than it appears, since no other company could replace the prestige China derives from the relationship.
key_points: The iPhone ban likely reflects both CCP nationalism and frustration that iPhone security prevents government surveillance of employees
Apple and China are locked in mutual dependence: Apple needs Chinese manufacturing while China gains prestige from producing the world's premier consumer product
China antagonizing Apple sends a damaging signal to other companies considering Chinese manufacturing: once dependent, you'll be leveraged against
Huawei is no real competitor to Apple — higher specs on paper don't translate to a better product, and everyone including Chinese consumers knows it
Bloomberg's institutional behavior reveals media pettiness around scoops, and their credibility remains damaged by the unretracted 'Big Hack' story
money_quotes: The Chinese government surely wants to surveil what government employees do on their phones, and iPhones make that harder.
iPhones aren't just the nicest phones in the world — they're arguably the nicest and most complex mass-produced consumer products in any category. And they are made almost exclusively in China.

If China maintains its symbiotic relationship with Apple, China will remain firmly associated with the most prestigious technology brand in the world. If not, and Apple migrates its primary manufacturing elsewhere, China again drops to being associated only with second- and third-rate products.
China gains enormous prestige from Apple; Apple takes a reputational hit from its reliance upon a brutal human-rights-violating communist dictatorship.
Once you grow dependent upon China we'll screw you like we screwed Apple.
This sandbagging of Apple regarding iPhone usage by government employees says the opposite: that China cannot be trusted as a partner.

If Apple does ease away from China, though, who can China replace them with? Who could they tout as a world-class technology company that relies upon China for manufacturing? Second place is so far behind Apple you can't see it.
"Resolution" is the last refuge of camera scoundrels.
key_insight: China's ban on iPhones for government employees risks undermining its own credibility as a manufacturing partner, because Apple confers more prestige on China than China confers on Apple.
